title: Synthetic Altermagnetism Beyond the Crystal Limit
authors: Rodolfo A. Gallardo, Andrea M. León, Jürgen Lindner, Jhon W. González
abstract: Altermagnetic magnons in crystalline materials exhibit momentum-dependent splitting whose nodal structure and chiral character are governed by the point-group symmetry of the magnetic sublattice rotation. Here, we demonstrate the first synthetic realization of altermagnetic magnonics in a continuum platform composed of antiferromagnetically coupled ferromagnetic films with alternating in-plane exchange anisotropies, showing that the key signatures of altermagnetic magnonics emerge beyond the crystalline setting. Solving the linearized Landau-Lifshitz equation within a dipole-exchange framework, we show that this architecture reproduces the characteristic momentum-dependent splitting, nodal directions, and anisotropic isofrequency contours of A-type altermagnets. Long-range dipolar interactions qualitatively reconstruct this exchange-driven spectrum by lifting the nominal nodal degeneracy, hybridizing opposite-chirality modes, and producing a finite, thickness-dependent wave-vector splitting along directions that are nodal in the exchange-only limit. Extending the bilayer to finite multilayers reveals that synthetic altermagnetism undergoes a parity-dependent reconstruction that separates surface and bulk altermagnetic excitations. These results establish altermagnetic magnon phenomenology as an engineerable collective response of dipole-exchange multilayers beyond microscopic crystal symmetries.
summary: The paper theoretically demonstrates how synthetic altermagnetism can be realized in multilayered magnetic films without requiring intrinsically altermagnetic crystals. By engineering alternating anisotropic exchange couplings, the authors show that magnon spectra exhibit characteristic splitting and nodal structures. This work establishes parity and surface termination as key design principles for controlling these collective quantum magnetic responses.
main_problem: To demonstrate a synthetic realization of altermagnetic magnonics beyond the constraints imposed by crystalline symmetries.
main_result: The authors show that engineered multilayer structures can reproduce characteristic altermagnetic magnon spectra, and this phenomenon is controllable via stack parity and surface termination effects.
method: Theoretical modeling involving solving the linearized Landau-Lifshitz equation within a dipole-exchange framework for coupled ferromagnetic multilayers.
model_or_system: The system is an antiferromagnetically coupled multilayer of ferromagnetic films with alternating in-plane exchange anisotropies, forming a continuum platform. The dynamics are governed by the LLG equation applied to spin-wave excitations across multiple layers.
key_observables: Momentum-dependent branch splitting ($\Delta k$), anisotropic isofrequency contours, and parity-dependent reconstruction of surface vs. bulk magnon modes.
important_parameters: Stacking layer number ($N$), spacer thickness, and the relative strength of dipole interactions versus exchange coupling.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the continuum approximation for anisotropic exchange and treats long-range dipolar interactions via magnetostatic formalism.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate spin-wave dispersion (a) showing momentum-dependent splitting, isofrequency contours (b) exhibiting anisotropy, and layer-resolved dynamics (c, d), with further figures detailing parity dependence in multilayers.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theoretical framework by defining the effective Hamiltonian/equations of motion for coupled layers, solving the eigenvalue problem for spin waves, analyzing the minimal bilayer case, and finally extending the analysis to finite multilayers to reveal parity-dependent reconstruction effects.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, material-science inspired model (multilayers) to study exotic quantum magnetic phenomena (altermagnetism), which is relevant for understanding collective excitations in engineered quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Synthetic altermagnetic bilayer composed of two antiferromagnetically coupled ferromagnetic layers (N = 2) with opposite equilibrium magnetizations. (a) Spin-wave dispersion along the high-symmetry path Y−Γ−X−M−Γ of the Brillouin zone. The inset illustrates the reciprocal-space directions and the wave-vector splitting ∆k. (b) Isofrequency contours in the (kx, kz) plane for excitation frequencies of 7 and 14 GHz. The contours exhibit a pronounced anisotropic deformation and a momentum-dependent separation of the two magnon branches, characteristic of altermagnetic order. The color scale represents the magnon polarization parameter χ, highlighting the opposite polarization character...

FIG. 2. Wave-vector separation ∆k along the Γ–M direction as a function of the magnetic layer thickness d for excitation frequencies of 10, 20, and 30 GHz. The increase of ∆k with thickness reflects the progressive enhancement of magneto- static effects in the dipole-exchange regime. Insets show the isofrequency contours at f = 10 GHz for two representative thicknesses, d = 0.5 nm and d = 10 nm. As the thickness increases, the contours become increasingly distorted due to the stronger influence of dipolar interactions, leading to an enhanced wave-vector separation along the Γ–M propagation direction.

FIG. 3. (a,b) Spin-wave dispersions for synthetic altermagnetic multilayers with odd numbers of magnetic layers, N = 9 and N = 29, respectively. (c,d) Corresponding spectra for even multilayers with N = 10 and N = 30. (e–h) Layer-resolved precessional trajectories of selected modes marked in panel (c) (modes n = 1, 2, 6 and 10 evaluated at the point X), represented through the normalized dynamical magnetization components (mx, mz) as a function of the layer index ν. The even and odd magnetization oscillations illustrate the sublattice-selective dynamical response that emerges in the synthetic altermagnetic phase.
